flashcards.front: What is dental caries? Describe the etiology of dental caries.
flashcards.back: **Dental caries** is a **microbial disease** of calcified tissues of teeth characterized by **demineralization** of inorganic portion and **destruction** of organic substance. It is a **chronic, progressive, multifactorial disease**. **Etiology (Multi-factorial)**: **1. Host factors (Tooth)**: Susceptible tooth surface, tooth morphology (deep pits and fissures), tooth position, enamel composition (fluoride content). **2. Microorganisms**: Primary: **Streptococcus mutans** (initiates caries), **L...

flashcards.back: **Newbrun's Triad**: Host + Microorganisms + Substrate overlapping with time factor

flashcards.front: Describe GV Black's classification of cavities.
flashcards.back: **GV Black's classification** (1908) based on **tooth surfaces involved**: **Class I**: **Pits and fissures** - Occlusal surfaces of molars/premolars, buccal/lingual surfaces of molars, lingual pit of maxillary incisors. **Class II**: **Proximal surfaces** of **posterior teeth** (molars/premolars). Subdivided: Compound (proximal + occlusal), Complex (proximal + occlusal + facial/lingual). **Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**. **Class IV**: **...

flashcards.front: Define: Gingival third
flashcards.back: **Class V**: **Gingival third** of **facial or lingual surfaces** of **all teeth**

flashcards.front: Define: anterior teeth
flashcards.back: **Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**

flashcards.front: Define: Class VI
flashcards.back: **Class VI** (Simon, 1956): **Incisal edges** of anteriors and **cusp tips** of posteriors

flashcards.front: What are the methods of diagnosing dental caries?
flashcards.back: **Methods of caries diagnosis**: **1. Visual examination**: Dry tooth surface, look for chalky white spots (early), brown/black discoloration (advanced), cavitation. **2. Tactile examination**: Gentle probing with blunt explorer (not sharp - may create iatrogenic cavitation). Feel for softness, stickiness. **3. Radiographic examination**: **Bitewing radiographs** best for interproximal caries, periapical for periapical pathology. **4. Trans-illumination**: Fiber-optic light detects proximal cari...

flashcards.front: What is the role of fluoride in preventing dental caries?
flashcards.back: **Fluoride prevents caries** through multiple mechanisms: **1. Remineralization**: Fluoride ions promote **remineralization** of early enamel lesions by forming **fluorapatite** which is more acid-resistant than hydroxyapatite. **2. Inhibits demineralization**: Reduces enamel solubility in acidic environment. **3. Antibacterial effect**: Inhibits **bacterial enzymes** (enolase) reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria. **Sources**: **Systemic**: Water fluoridation (**0.7-1.2 ppm**), fluor...

flashcards.front: What are pit and fissure sealants? Describe their indications and procedure.
flashcards.back: **Pit and fissure sealants** are **resin-based materials** placed in pits and fissures to prevent caries by creating a **physical barrier**. **Indications**: (1) **Deep pits and fissures** in caries-susceptible teeth, (2) **Recently erupted** molars/premolars in high-risk patients, (3) **Occlusal surfaces** without caries or small enamel caries. **Contraindications**: Frank cavitation, adequate self-cleansing surfaces, good oral hygiene with low caries risk. **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - ...

flashcards.front: Define: Clean tooth
flashcards.back: **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - pumice prophylaxis, (2) **Isolate** - rubber dam or cotton rolls, (3) **Etch** - 37% phosphoric acid for 15-30 seconds, (4) **Rinse and dry** thoroughly (chalky white appearance), (5) **Apply sealant** - light-cure or auto-polymerizing resin, (6) **Light cure** if photo-activated, (7) **Check occlusion** - adjust high spots

flashcards.front: Define: Rinse and dry
flashcards.back: **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - pumice prophylaxis, (2) **Isolate** - rubber dam or cotton rolls, (3) **Etch** - 37% phosphoric acid for 15-30 seconds, (4) **Rinse and dry** thoroughly (chalky white appearance), (5) **Apply sealant** - light-cure or auto-polymerizing resin, (6) **Light cure** if photo-activated, (7) **Check occlusion** - adjust high spots

flashcards.front: What are the principles of cavity preparation?
flashcards.back: **GV Black's principles of cavity preparation**: **1. Outline form**: Initial depth and external shape of preparation. Remove all caries, include defective enamel, extend to self-cleansing areas. **2. Resistance form**: Features that prevent restoration and tooth fracture under occlusal forces. Achieved by: (a) Flat pulpal and gingival floors perpendicular to forces, (b) Adequate dentin support for enamel, (c) Proper thickness for restoration. **3. Retention form**: Features preventing restorati...

flashcards.front: Define cavity nomenclature: walls, floors, line angles, point angles, and cavosurface angle.
flashcards.back: **Cavity nomenclature**: **Walls**: Prepared vertical surfaces named after adjacent tooth surface - **Mesial, Distal, Buccal/Facial, Lingual/Palatal** walls. **Floors**: Prepared horizontal surfaces - **Pulpal floor** (perpendicular to long axis over pulp), **Gingival floor** (in Class II), **Axial wall** (parallel to long axis, internal wall of proximal box). **Line angles**: Junction of **two walls/floors** - named by combining wall names (e.g., **mesiobuccal, distolingual, pulpoaxial**). **Po...

flashcards.front: What is a matrix band? Describe the types and procedure of matrix placement.
flashcards.back: A **matrix band** is a **thin metal or plastic strip** placed around a tooth to provide a **temporary wall** for proximal surface during restoration, allowing proper contour and contact. **Types**: **1. Tofflemire matrix** (Universal): Most common for **Class II** restorations. Components: Retainer, matrix band (straight or contoured). **2. Automatrix**: Circular band, self-tightening, no retainer needed. **3. Sectional matrix (Palodent)**: For **composite** restorations, provides better contact...

flashcards.front: Define: Place wooden wedge
flashcards.back: **Tofflemire procedure**: (1) **Select band** - appropriate width and contour, (2) **Assemble** in retainer with gingival edge toward gingiva, (3) **Place** around tooth, adapt to proximal surface, (4) **Tighten** - band should be snug, (5) **Place wooden wedge** interproximally to (a) seal gingival margin, (b) separate teeth slightly, (c) prevent overhang, (6) **Burnish** band against adjacent tooth for contact, (7) **Remove after restoration** sets

flashcards.front: What is a rubber dam? What are its advantages?
flashcards.back: **Rubber dam** is a **thin latex or non-latex sheet** used to **isolate teeth** from oral environment during dental procedures. **Components**: Rubber dam sheet, punch, clamps, frame, forceps, floss. **Advantages**: **1. Moisture control**: Keeps field dry, essential for composite bonding, endodontics. **2. Infection control**: Prevents contamination from saliva, blood. **3. Soft tissue protection**: Protects cheeks, tongue, gingiva from instruments, materials. **4. Improves access and visibilit...

flashcards.front: Differentiate between cavity liners and bases. When are they indicated?
flashcards.back: **Cavity liner**: **Thin coating** (0.5mm or less) applied to **dentinal walls** to provide **pulpal protection**. **Materials**: **Calcium hydroxide**, **Glass ionomer**, **Resin-modified glass ionomer**, **Varnish** (copal varnish). **Functions**: (1) **Therapeutic** - stimulates secondary dentin formation (Ca(OH)₂), (2) **Protective** - bacterial seal, (3) **Palliative** - reduces sensitivity. **Cavity base**: **Thicker layer** (>0.5mm, up to 1-2mm) providing **thermal insulation** and **mech...

flashcards.back: **Functions**: (1) **Thermal insulation** - protects pulp from metallic restorations, (2) **Mechanical protection** - distributes stress, (3) **Thickness replacement** - in deep cavities

key_points: **Dental caries** is a **microbial disease** of calcified tissues of teeth characterized by **demineralization** of inorganic portion and **destruction** of organic substance
It is a **chronic, progressive, multifactorial disease**
**Etiology (Multi-factorial)**: **1
Host factors (Tooth)**: Susceptible tooth surface, tooth morphology (deep pits and fissures), tooth position, enamel composition (fluoride content)
Microorganisms**: Primary: **Streptococcus mutans** (initiates caries), **Lactobacillus** (progression)

**GV Black's classification** (1908) based on **tooth surfaces involved**: **Class I**: **Pits and fissures** - Occlusal surfaces of molars/premolars, buccal/lingual surfaces of molars, lingual pit of maxillary incisors
**Class II**: **Proximal surfaces** of **posterior teeth** (molars/premolars)
Subdivided: Compound (proximal + occlusal), Complex (proximal + occlusal + facial/lingual)
**Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**
**Class IV**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **involving incisal angle/edge**
**Methods of caries diagnosis**: **1
Visual examination**: Dry tooth surface, look for chalky white spots (early), brown/black discoloration (advanced), cavitation
Tactile examination**: Gentle probing with blunt explorer (not sharp - may create iatrogenic cavitation)
Feel for softness, stickiness
Radiographic examination**: **Bitewing radiographs** best for interproximal caries, periapical for periapical pathology
**Fluoride prevents caries** through multiple mechanisms: **1
Remineralization**: Fluoride ions promote **remineralization** of early enamel lesions by forming **fluorapatite** which is more acid-resistant than hydroxyapatite
Inhibits demineralization**: Reduces enamel solubility in acidic environment
Antibacterial effect**: Inhibits **bacterial enzymes** (enolase) reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria
**Sources**: **Systemic**: Water fluoridation (**0
Deep pits and fissures: in caries-susceptible teeth
Recently erupted: molars/premolars in high-risk patients
Occlusal surfaces: without caries or small enamel caries. **Contraindications**: Frank cavitation, adequate self-cleansing surfaces, good oral hygiene with low caries risk. **Procedure**:
Clean tooth: pumice prophylaxis
Isolate: rubber dam or cotton rolls
clinical_pearls: high spots. **Materials**: Resin-based (most common), glass ionomer (in difficult isolation). **Retention**: 90% at 1 year, require

key_points: **GV Black's principles of cavity preparation**: **1
Outline form**: Initial depth and external shape of preparation
Remove all caries, include defective enamel, extend to self-cleansing areas
Resistance form**: Features that prevent restoration and tooth fracture under occlusal forces
Achieved by: (a) Flat pulpal and gingival floors perpendicular to forces, (b) Adequate dentin support for enamel, (c) Proper thickness for restoration
**Cavity nomenclature**: **Walls**: Prepared vertical surfaces named after adjacent tooth surface - **Mesial, Distal, Buccal/Facial, Lingual/Palatal** walls

**Floors**: Prepared horizontal surfaces - **Pulpal floor** (perpendicular to long axis over pulp), **Gingival floor** (in Class II), **Axial wall** (parallel to long axis, internal wall of proximal box)
**Line angles**: Junction of **two walls/floors** - named by combining wall names (e
, **mesiobuccal, distolingual, pulpoaxial**)
**Point angles**: Junction of **three walls/floors** (e
Select band: appropriate width and contour
Assemble: in retainer with gingival edge toward gingiva
Place: around tooth, adapt to proximal surface
Tighten: band should be snug
Place wooden wedge: interproximally to (a) seal gingival margin, (b) separate teeth slightly, (c) prevent overhang
Burnish: band against adjacent tooth for contact
Remove after restoration: sets.
**Rubber dam** is a **thin latex or non-latex sheet** used to **isolate teeth** from oral environment during dental procedures
**Components**: Rubber dam sheet, punch, clamps, frame, forceps, floss
Moisture control**: Keeps field dry, essential for composite bonding, endodontics
Infection control**: Prevents contamination from saliva, blood
Soft tissue protection**: Protects cheeks, tongue, gingiva from instruments, materials
Therapeutic: stimulates secondary dentin formation (Ca(OH)₂)
Protective: bacterial seal

Palliative: reduces sensitivity. **Cavity base**: **Thicker layer** (>0.5mm, up to 1-2mm) providing **thermal insulation** and **mechanical support**. **Materials**: **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)**, **Zinc phosphate cement**, **Glass ionomer cement**, **Resin-modified GI**. **Functions**:

long_essay.question: What is dental caries? Describe the etiology of dental caries.
long_essay.model_answer: **Dental caries** is a **microbial disease** of calcified tissues of teeth characterized by **demineralization** of inorganic portion and **destruction** of organic substance. It is a **chronic, progressive, multifactorial disease**. **Etiology (Multi-factorial)**: **1. Host factors (Tooth)**: Susceptible tooth surface, tooth morphology (deep pits and fissures), tooth position, enamel composition (fluoride content). **2. Microorganisms**: Primary: **Streptococcus mutans** (initiates caries), **Lactobacillus** (progression). Bacteria form **biofilm (dental plaque)** metabolizing sugars. **3. Substrate (Diet)**: **Fermentable carbohydrates** metabolized producing **organic acids** lowering pH below **critical pH (5.5)** causing demineralization. **Frequency** of sugar intake more critical than amount. **4. Time**: Adequate time for acid production and demineralization. **Newbrun's Triad**: Host + Microorganisms + Substrate overlapping with time factor.

long_essay.question: Describe GV Black's classification of cavities.
long_essay.model_answer: **GV Black's classification** (1908) based on **tooth surfaces involved**: **Class I**: **Pits and fissures** - Occlusal surfaces of molars/premolars, buccal/lingual surfaces of molars, lingual pit of maxillary incisors. **Class II**: **Proximal surfaces** of **posterior teeth** (molars/premolars). Subdivided: Compound (proximal + occlusal), Complex (proximal + occlusal + facial/lingual). **Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**. **Class IV**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **involving incisal angle/edge**. **Class V**: **Gingival third** of **facial or lingual surfaces** of **all teeth**. **Class VI** (Simon, 1956): **Incisal edges** of anteriors and **cusp tips** of posteriors. Classification guides cavity design, material selection, treatment planning.

long_essay.question: What are the methods of diagnosing dental caries?
long_essay.model_answer: **Methods of caries diagnosis**: **1. Visual examination**: Dry tooth surface, look for chalky white spots (early), brown/black discoloration (advanced), cavitation. **2. Tactile examination**: Gentle probing with blunt explorer (not sharp - may create iatrogenic cavitation). Feel for softness, stickiness. **3. Radiographic examination**: **Bitewing radiographs** best for interproximal caries, periapical for periapical pathology. **4. Trans-illumination**: Fiber-optic light detects proximal caries. **5. Caries detector dyes**: Stains infected dentin only (red/blue dye). **6. Laser fluorescence (DIAGNOdent)**: Detects caries by measuring fluorescence. **7. Electrical conductance**: Measures changes in electrical resistance. **8. Digital imaging fiber-optic trans-illumination (DIFOTI)**. Early diagnosis prevents progression, allows minimally invasive treatment.

long_essay.question: What is the role of fluoride in preventing dental caries?
long_essay.model_answer: **Fluoride prevents caries** through multiple mechanisms: **1. Remineralization**: Fluoride ions promote **remineralization** of early enamel lesions by forming **fluorapatite** which is more acid-resistant than hydroxyapatite. **2. Inhibits demineralization**: Reduces enamel solubility in acidic environment. **3. Antibacterial effect**: Inhibits **bacterial enzymes** (enolase) reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria. **Sources**: **Systemic**: Water fluoridation (**0.7-1.2 ppm**), fluoride supplements. **Topical**: Fluoride toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm), mouth rinses (0.05% NaF daily, 0.2% weekly), professional application (1.23% APF gel, 2% NaF, 5% NaF varnish). **Optimal effect**: Combination of systemic and topical. **Fluorosis risk**: Excessive intake during tooth development causes enamel fluorosis.

long_essay.question: What are pit and fissure sealants? Describe their indications and procedure.
long_essay.model_answer: **Pit and fissure sealants** are **resin-based materials** placed in pits and fissures to prevent caries by creating a **physical barrier**. **Indications**: (1) **Deep pits and fissures** in caries-susceptible teeth, (2) **Recently erupted** molars/premolars in high-risk patients, (3) **Occlusal surfaces** without caries or small enamel caries. **Contraindications**: Frank cavitation, adequate self-cleansing surfaces, good oral hygiene with low caries risk. **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - pumice prophylaxis, (2) **Isolate** - rubber dam or cotton rolls, (3) **Etch** - 37% phosphoric acid for 15-30 seconds, (4) **Rinse and dry** thoroughly (chalky white appearance), (5) **Apply sealant** - light-cure or auto-polymerizing resin, (6) **Light cure** if photo-activated, (7) **Check occlusion** - adjust high spots. **Materials**: Resin-based (most common), glass ionomer (in difficult isolation). **Retention**: 90% at 1 year, requires periodic evaluation.

long_essay.question: What are the principles of cavity preparation?
long_essay.model_answer: **GV Black's principles of cavity preparation**: **1. Outline form**: Initial depth and external shape of preparation. Remove all caries, include defective enamel, extend to self-cleansing areas. **2. Resistance form**: Features that prevent restoration and tooth fracture under occlusal forces. Achieved by: (a) Flat pulpal and gingival floors perpendicular to forces, (b) Adequate dentin support for enamel, (c) Proper thickness for restoration. **3. Retention form**: Features preventing restoration displacement. Achieved by: (a) **Undercuts** (for amalgam), (b) **Parallel walls** with slight convergence, (c) **Adequate depth** (1.5-2mm for amalgam), (d) **Bonding** (for composite). **4. Convenience form**: Features facilitating cavity preparation and restoration placement. **5. Removal of remaining caries**: Complete excavation. **6. Finishing enamel walls**: Smooth margins, remove unsupported enamel. **7. Toilet of cavity**: Clean debris. **Modern modification**: **Minimal intervention** - preserve maximum tooth structure, use adhesive materials, extension for prevention no longer followed.

long_essay.question: Define cavity nomenclature: walls, floors, line angles, point angles, and cavosurface angle.
long_essay.model_answer: **Cavity nomenclature**: **Walls**: Prepared vertical surfaces named after adjacent tooth surface - **Mesial, Distal, Buccal/Facial, Lingual/Palatal** walls. **Floors**: Prepared horizontal surfaces - **Pulpal floor** (perpendicular to long axis over pulp), **Gingival floor** (in Class II), **Axial wall** (parallel to long axis, internal wall of proximal box). **Line angles**: Junction of **two walls/floors** - named by combining wall names (e.g., **mesiobuccal, distolingual, pulpoaxial**). **Point angles**: Junction of **three walls/floors** (e.g., **mesiobucopulpal**, **distolinguopulpal**). **Cavosurface angle/margin**: Angle formed by junction of **prepared cavity wall** with **unprepared tooth surface**. Ideally **90 degrees for amalgam**, **butt joint for composite**. **Cavo**: cavity, **surface**: external tooth surface.

long_essay.question: What is a matrix band? Describe the types and procedure of matrix placement.
long_essay.model_answer: A **matrix band** is a **thin metal or plastic strip** placed around a tooth to provide a **temporary wall** for proximal surface during restoration, allowing proper contour and contact. **Types**: **1. Tofflemire matrix** (Universal): Most common for **Class II** restorations. Components: Retainer, matrix band (straight or contoured). **2. Automatrix**: Circular band, self-tightening, no retainer needed. **3. Sectional matrix (Palodent)**: For **composite** restorations, provides better contact and contour. Uses separation ring. **4. Celluloid/Mylar strip**: For **anterior** Class III/IV composite. **5. Copper band**: For extensively damaged teeth. **Tofflemire procedure**: (1) **Select band** - appropriate width and contour, (2) **Assemble** in retainer with gingival edge toward gingiva, (3) **Place** around tooth, adapt to proximal surface, (4) **Tighten** - band should be snug, (5) **Place wooden wedge** interproximally to (a) seal gingival margin, (b) separate teeth slightly, (c) prevent overhang, (6) **Burnish** band against adjacent tooth for contact, (7) **Remove after restoration** sets.

long_essay.question: What is a rubber dam? What are its advantages?
long_essay.model_answer: **Rubber dam** is a **thin latex or non-latex sheet** used to **isolate teeth** from oral environment during dental procedures. **Components**: Rubber dam sheet, punch, clamps, frame, forceps, floss. **Advantages**: **1. Moisture control**: Keeps field dry, essential for composite bonding, endodontics. **2. Infection control**: Prevents contamination from saliva, blood. **3. Soft tissue protection**: Protects cheeks, tongue, gingiva from instruments, materials. **4. Improves access and visibility**: Retracts soft tissues, better illumination. **5. Patient safety**: Prevents aspiration/ingestion of instruments, materials, irrigants. **6. Improved restoration quality**: Better bond strength for composites. **7. Patient comfort**: No constant rinsing/spitting, less gagging. **8. Increases efficiency**: Fewer interruptions. **Absolute indication**: **Endodontic treatment** (prevents aspiration of files, irrigant leakage). **Contraindications**: Severe asthma (latex allergy - use non-latex), mouth breathing difficulty.

long_essay.question: Differentiate between cavity liners and bases. When are they indicated?
long_essay.model_answer: **Cavity liner**: **Thin coating** (0.5mm or less) applied to **dentinal walls** to provide **pulpal protection**. **Materials**: **Calcium hydroxide**, **Glass ionomer**, **Resin-modified glass ionomer**, **Varnish** (copal varnish). **Functions**: (1) **Therapeutic** - stimulates secondary dentin formation (Ca(OH)₂), (2) **Protective** - bacterial seal, (3) **Palliative** - reduces sensitivity. **Cavity base**: **Thicker layer** (>0.5mm, up to 1-2mm) providing **thermal insulation** and **mechanical support**. **Materials**: **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)**, **Zinc phosphate cement**, **Glass ionomer cement**, **Resin-modified GI**. **Functions**: (1) **Thermal insulation** - protects pulp from metallic restorations, (2) **Mechanical protection** - distributes stress, (3) **Thickness replacement** - in deep cavities. **Indications**: **Deep cavities** (within 0.5mm of pulp), **Sensitive dentin**, **Under metallic restorations**. **Modern approach**: With **adhesive dentistry** (composites with bonding), extensive bases/liners often not needed. **Glass ionomer liner** releases fluoride, bonds to dentin. **Direct composite** over dentin with bonding agent eliminates need for base in many cases.

short_answer.question: What is dental caries? Describe the etiology of dental caries.
short_answer.model_answer: **Dental caries** is a **microbial disease** of calcified tissues of teeth characterized by **demineralization** of inorganic portion and **destruction** of organic substance. It is a **chronic, progressive, multifactorial disease**. **Etiology (Multi-factorial)**: **1. Host factors (Tooth)**: Susceptible tooth surface, tooth morphology (deep pits and fissures), tooth position, enamel composition (fluoride content). **2. Microorganisms**: Primary: **Streptococcus mutans** (initiates caries), **Lactobacillus** (progression). Bacteria form **biofilm (dental plaque)** metabolizing sugars. **3. Substrate (Diet)**: **Fermentable carbohydrates** metabolized producing **organic acids** lowering pH below **critical pH (5.5)** causing demineralization. **Frequency** of sugar intake more critical than amount. **4. Time**: Adequate time for acid production and demineralization. **Newbrun's Triad**: Host + Microorganisms + Substrate overlapping with time factor.

short_answer.question: Describe GV Black's classification of cavities.
short_answer.model_answer: **GV Black's classification** (1908) based on **tooth surfaces involved**: **Class I**: **Pits and fissures** - Occlusal surfaces of molars/premolars, buccal/lingual surfaces of molars, lingual pit of maxillary incisors. **Class II**: **Proximal surfaces** of **posterior teeth** (molars/premolars). Subdivided: Compound (proximal + occlusal), Complex (proximal + occlusal + facial/lingual). **Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**. **Class IV**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **involving incisal angle/edge**. **Class V**: **Gingival third** of **facial or lingual surfaces** of **all teeth**. **Class VI** (Simon, 1956): **Incisal edges** of anteriors and **cusp tips** of posteriors. Classification guides cavity design, material selection, treatment planning.

short_answer.question: What are the methods of diagnosing dental caries?
short_answer.model_answer: **Methods of caries diagnosis**: **1. Visual examination**: Dry tooth surface, look for chalky white spots (early), brown/black discoloration (advanced), cavitation. **2. Tactile examination**: Gentle probing with blunt explorer (not sharp - may create iatrogenic cavitation). Feel for softness, stickiness. **3. Radiographic examination**: **Bitewing radiographs** best for interproximal caries, periapical for periapical pathology. **4. Trans-illumination**: Fiber-optic light detects proximal caries. **5. Caries detector dyes**: Stains infected dentin only (red/blue dye). **6. Laser fluorescence (DIAGNOdent)**: Detects caries by measuring fluorescence. **7. Electrical conductance**: Measures changes in electrical resistance. **8. Digital imaging fiber-optic trans-illumination (DIFOTI)**. Early diagnosis prevents progression, allows minimally invasive treatment.

short_answer.question: What is the role of fluoride in preventing dental caries?
short_answer.model_answer: **Fluoride prevents caries** through multiple mechanisms: **1. Remineralization**: Fluoride ions promote **remineralization** of early enamel lesions by forming **fluorapatite** which is more acid-resistant than hydroxyapatite. **2. Inhibits demineralization**: Reduces enamel solubility in acidic environment. **3. Antibacterial effect**: Inhibits **bacterial enzymes** (enolase) reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria. **Sources**: **Systemic**: Water fluoridation (**0.7-1.2 ppm**), fluoride supplements. **Topical**: Fluoride toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm), mouth rinses (0.05% NaF daily, 0.2% weekly), professional application (1.23% APF gel, 2% NaF, 5% NaF varnish). **Optimal effect**: Combination of systemic and topical. **Fluorosis risk**: Excessive intake during tooth development causes enamel fluorosis.

short_answer.question: What are pit and fissure sealants? Describe their indications and procedure.
short_answer.model_answer: **Pit and fissure sealants** are **resin-based materials** placed in pits and fissures to prevent caries by creating a **physical barrier**. **Indications**: (1) **Deep pits and fissures** in caries-susceptible teeth, (2) **Recently erupted** molars/premolars in high-risk patients, (3) **Occlusal surfaces** without caries or small enamel caries. **Contraindications**: Frank cavitation, adequate self-cleansing surfaces, good oral hygiene with low caries risk. **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - pumice prophylaxis, (2) **Isolate** - rubber dam or cotton rolls, (3) **Etch** - 37% phosphoric acid for 15-30 seconds, (4) **Rinse and dry** thoroughly (chalky white appearance), (5) **Apply sealant** - light-cure or auto-polymerizing resin, (6) **Light cure** if photo-activated, (7) **Check occlusion** - adjust high spots. **Materials**: Resin-based (most common), glass ionomer (in difficult isolation). **Retention**: 90% at 1 year, requires periodic evaluation.

short_answer.question: What are the principles of cavity preparation?
short_answer.model_answer: **GV Black's principles of cavity preparation**: **1. Outline form**: Initial depth and external shape of preparation. Remove all caries, include defective enamel, extend to self-cleansing areas. **2. Resistance form**: Features that prevent restoration and tooth fracture under occlusal forces. Achieved by: (a) Flat pulpal and gingival floors perpendicular to forces, (b) Adequate dentin support for enamel, (c) Proper thickness for restoration. **3. Retention form**: Features preventing restoration displacement. Achieved by: (a) **Undercuts** (for amalgam), (b) **Parallel walls** with slight convergence, (c) **Adequate depth** (1.5-2mm for amalgam), (d) **Bonding** (for composite). **4. Convenience form**: Features facilitating cavity preparation and restoration placement. **5. Removal of remaining caries**: Complete excavation. **6. Finishing enamel walls**: Smooth margins, remove unsupported enamel. **7. Toilet of cavity**: Clean debris. **Modern modification**: **Minimal intervention** - preserve maximum tooth structure, use adhesive materials, extension for prevention no longer followed.

short_answer.question: Define cavity nomenclature: walls, floors, line angles, point angles, and cavosurface angle.
short_answer.model_answer: **Cavity nomenclature**: **Walls**: Prepared vertical surfaces named after adjacent tooth surface - **Mesial, Distal, Buccal/Facial, Lingual/Palatal** walls. **Floors**: Prepared horizontal surfaces - **Pulpal floor** (perpendicular to long axis over pulp), **Gingival floor** (in Class II), **Axial wall** (parallel to long axis, internal wall of proximal box). **Line angles**: Junction of **two walls/floors** - named by combining wall names (e.g., **mesiobuccal, distolingual, pulpoaxial**). **Point angles**: Junction of **three walls/floors** (e.g., **mesiobucopulpal**, **distolinguopulpal**). **Cavosurface angle/margin**: Angle formed by junction of **prepared cavity wall** with **unprepared tooth surface**. Ideally **90 degrees for amalgam**, **butt joint for composite**. **Cavo**: cavity, **surface**: external tooth surface.

short_answer.question: What is a matrix band? Describe the types and procedure of matrix placement.
short_answer.model_answer: A **matrix band** is a **thin metal or plastic strip** placed around a tooth to provide a **temporary wall** for proximal surface during restoration, allowing proper contour and contact. **Types**: **1. Tofflemire matrix** (Universal): Most common for **Class II** restorations. Components: Retainer, matrix band (straight or contoured). **2. Automatrix**: Circular band, self-tightening, no retainer needed. **3. Sectional matrix (Palodent)**: For **composite** restorations, provides better contact and contour. Uses separation ring. **4. Celluloid/Mylar strip**: For **anterior** Class III/IV composite. **5. Copper band**: For extensively damaged teeth. **Tofflemire procedure**: (1) **Select band** - appropriate width and contour, (2) **Assemble** in retainer with gingival edge toward gingiva, (3) **Place** around tooth, adapt to proximal surface, (4) **Tighten** - band should be snug, (5) **Place wooden wedge** interproximally to (a) seal gingival margin, (b) separate teeth slightly, (c) prevent overhang, (6) **Burnish** band against adjacent tooth for contact, (7) **Remove after restoration** sets.

short_answer.question: What is a rubber dam? What are its advantages?
short_answer.model_answer: **Rubber dam** is a **thin latex or non-latex sheet** used to **isolate teeth** from oral environment during dental procedures. **Components**: Rubber dam sheet, punch, clamps, frame, forceps, floss. **Advantages**: **1. Moisture control**: Keeps field dry, essential for composite bonding, endodontics. **2. Infection control**: Prevents contamination from saliva, blood. **3. Soft tissue protection**: Protects cheeks, tongue, gingiva from instruments, materials. **4. Improves access and visibility**: Retracts soft tissues, better illumination. **5. Patient safety**: Prevents aspiration/ingestion of instruments, materials, irrigants. **6. Improved restoration quality**: Better bond strength for composites. **7. Patient comfort**: No constant rinsing/spitting, less gagging. **8. Increases efficiency**: Fewer interruptions. **Absolute indication**: **Endodontic treatment** (prevents aspiration of files, irrigant leakage). **Contraindications**: Severe asthma (latex allergy - use non-latex), mouth breathing difficulty.

short_answer.question: Differentiate between cavity liners and bases. When are they indicated?
short_answer.model_answer: **Cavity liner**: **Thin coating** (0.5mm or less) applied to **dentinal walls** to provide **pulpal protection**. **Materials**: **Calcium hydroxide**, **Glass ionomer**, **Resin-modified glass ionomer**, **Varnish** (copal varnish). **Functions**: (1) **Therapeutic** - stimulates secondary dentin formation (Ca(OH)₂), (2) **Protective** - bacterial seal, (3) **Palliative** - reduces sensitivity. **Cavity base**: **Thicker layer** (>0.5mm, up to 1-2mm) providing **thermal insulation** and **mechanical support**. **Materials**: **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)**, **Zinc phosphate cement**, **Glass ionomer cement**, **Resin-modified GI**. **Functions**: (1) **Thermal insulation** - protects pulp from metallic restorations, (2) **Mechanical protection** - distributes stress, (3) **Thickness replacement** - in deep cavities. **Indications**: **Deep cavities** (within 0.5mm of pulp), **Sensitive dentin**, **Under metallic restorations**. **Modern approach**: With **adhesive dentistry** (composites with bonding), extensive bases/liners often not needed. **Glass ionomer liner** releases fluoride, bonds to dentin. **Direct composite** over dentin with bonding agent eliminates need for base in many cases.

viva.question: What is dental caries? Describe the etiology of dental caries.
viva.model_answer: **Dental caries** is a **microbial disease** of calcified tissues of teeth characterized by **demineralization** of inorganic portion and **destruction** of organic substance. It is a **chronic, progressive, multifactorial disease**. **Etiology (Multi-factorial)**: **1. Host factors (Tooth)**: Susceptible tooth surface, tooth morphology (deep pits and fissures), tooth position, enamel composition (fluoride content). **2. Microorganisms**: Primary: **Streptococcus mutans** (initiates caries), **Lactobacillus** (progression). Bacteria form **biofilm (dental plaque)** metabolizing sugars. **3. Substrate (Diet)**: **Fermentable carbohydrates** metabolized producing **organic acids** lowering pH below **critical pH (5.5)** causing demineralization. **Frequency** of sugar intake more critical than amount. **4. Time**: Adequate time for acid production and demineralization. **Newbrun's Triad**: Host + Microorganisms + Substrate overlapping with time factor.

viva.question: Describe GV Black's classification of cavities.
viva.model_answer: **GV Black's classification** (1908) based on **tooth surfaces involved**: **Class I**: **Pits and fissures** - Occlusal surfaces of molars/premolars, buccal/lingual surfaces of molars, lingual pit of maxillary incisors. **Class II**: **Proximal surfaces** of **posterior teeth** (molars/premolars). Subdivided: Compound (proximal + occlusal), Complex (proximal + occlusal + facial/lingual). **Class III**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **NOT involving incisal angle**. **Class IV**: **Proximal surfaces** of **anterior teeth** **involving incisal angle/edge**. **Class V**: **Gingival third** of **facial or lingual surfaces** of **all teeth**. **Class VI** (Simon, 1956): **Incisal edges** of anteriors and **cusp tips** of posteriors. Classification guides cavity design, material selection, treatment planning.

viva.question: What are the methods of diagnosing dental caries?
viva.model_answer: **Methods of caries diagnosis**: **1. Visual examination**: Dry tooth surface, look for chalky white spots (early), brown/black discoloration (advanced), cavitation. **2. Tactile examination**: Gentle probing with blunt explorer (not sharp - may create iatrogenic cavitation). Feel for softness, stickiness. **3. Radiographic examination**: **Bitewing radiographs** best for interproximal caries, periapical for periapical pathology. **4. Trans-illumination**: Fiber-optic light detects proximal caries. **5. Caries detector dyes**: Stains infected dentin only (red/blue dye). **6. Laser fluorescence (DIAGNOdent)**: Detects caries by measuring fluorescence. **7. Electrical conductance**: Measures changes in electrical resistance. **8. Digital imaging fiber-optic trans-illumination (DIFOTI)**. Early diagnosis prevents progression, allows minimally invasive treatment.

viva.question: What is the role of fluoride in preventing dental caries?
viva.model_answer: **Fluoride prevents caries** through multiple mechanisms: **1. Remineralization**: Fluoride ions promote **remineralization** of early enamel lesions by forming **fluorapatite** which is more acid-resistant than hydroxyapatite. **2. Inhibits demineralization**: Reduces enamel solubility in acidic environment. **3. Antibacterial effect**: Inhibits **bacterial enzymes** (enolase) reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria. **Sources**: **Systemic**: Water fluoridation (**0.7-1.2 ppm**), fluoride supplements. **Topical**: Fluoride toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm), mouth rinses (0.05% NaF daily, 0.2% weekly), professional application (1.23% APF gel, 2% NaF, 5% NaF varnish). **Optimal effect**: Combination of systemic and topical. **Fluorosis risk**: Excessive intake during tooth development causes enamel fluorosis.

viva.question: What are pit and fissure sealants? Describe their indications and procedure.
viva.model_answer: **Pit and fissure sealants** are **resin-based materials** placed in pits and fissures to prevent caries by creating a **physical barrier**. **Indications**: (1) **Deep pits and fissures** in caries-susceptible teeth, (2) **Recently erupted** molars/premolars in high-risk patients, (3) **Occlusal surfaces** without caries or small enamel caries. **Contraindications**: Frank cavitation, adequate self-cleansing surfaces, good oral hygiene with low caries risk. **Procedure**: (1) **Clean tooth** - pumice prophylaxis, (2) **Isolate** - rubber dam or cotton rolls, (3) **Etch** - 37% phosphoric acid for 15-30 seconds, (4) **Rinse and dry** thoroughly (chalky white appearance), (5) **Apply sealant** - light-cure or auto-polymerizing resin, (6) **Light cure** if photo-activated, (7) **Check occlusion** - adjust high spots. **Materials**: Resin-based (most common), glass ionomer (in difficult isolation). **Retention**: 90% at 1 year, requires periodic evaluation.

viva.question: What are the principles of cavity preparation?
viva.model_answer: **GV Black's principles of cavity preparation**: **1. Outline form**: Initial depth and external shape of preparation. Remove all caries, include defective enamel, extend to self-cleansing areas. **2. Resistance form**: Features that prevent restoration and tooth fracture under occlusal forces. Achieved by: (a) Flat pulpal and gingival floors perpendicular to forces, (b) Adequate dentin support for enamel, (c) Proper thickness for restoration. **3. Retention form**: Features preventing restoration displacement. Achieved by: (a) **Undercuts** (for amalgam), (b) **Parallel walls** with slight convergence, (c) **Adequate depth** (1.5-2mm for amalgam), (d) **Bonding** (for composite). **4. Convenience form**: Features facilitating cavity preparation and restoration placement. **5. Removal of remaining caries**: Complete excavation. **6. Finishing enamel walls**: Smooth margins, remove unsupported enamel. **7. Toilet of cavity**: Clean debris. **Modern modification**: **Minimal intervention** - preserve maximum tooth structure, use adhesive materials, extension for prevention no longer followed.

viva.question: Define cavity nomenclature: walls, floors, line angles, point angles, and cavosurface angle.
viva.model_answer: **Cavity nomenclature**: **Walls**: Prepared vertical surfaces named after adjacent tooth surface - **Mesial, Distal, Buccal/Facial, Lingual/Palatal** walls. **Floors**: Prepared horizontal surfaces - **Pulpal floor** (perpendicular to long axis over pulp), **Gingival floor** (in Class II), **Axial wall** (parallel to long axis, internal wall of proximal box). **Line angles**: Junction of **two walls/floors** - named by combining wall names (e.g., **mesiobuccal, distolingual, pulpoaxial**). **Point angles**: Junction of **three walls/floors** (e.g., **mesiobucopulpal**, **distolinguopulpal**). **Cavosurface angle/margin**: Angle formed by junction of **prepared cavity wall** with **unprepared tooth surface**. Ideally **90 degrees for amalgam**, **butt joint for composite**. **Cavo**: cavity, **surface**: external tooth surface.

viva.question: What is a matrix band? Describe the types and procedure of matrix placement.
viva.model_answer: A **matrix band** is a **thin metal or plastic strip** placed around a tooth to provide a **temporary wall** for proximal surface during restoration, allowing proper contour and contact. **Types**: **1. Tofflemire matrix** (Universal): Most common for **Class II** restorations. Components: Retainer, matrix band (straight or contoured). **2. Automatrix**: Circular band, self-tightening, no retainer needed. **3. Sectional matrix (Palodent)**: For **composite** restorations, provides better contact and contour. Uses separation ring. **4. Celluloid/Mylar strip**: For **anterior** Class III/IV composite. **5. Copper band**: For extensively damaged teeth. **Tofflemire procedure**: (1) **Select band** - appropriate width and contour, (2) **Assemble** in retainer with gingival edge toward gingiva, (3) **Place** around tooth, adapt to proximal surface, (4) **Tighten** - band should be snug, (5) **Place wooden wedge** interproximally to (a) seal gingival margin, (b) separate teeth slightly, (c) prevent overhang, (6) **Burnish** band against adjacent tooth for contact, (7) **Remove after restoration** sets.

viva.question: What is a rubber dam? What are its advantages?
viva.model_answer: **Rubber dam** is a **thin latex or non-latex sheet** used to **isolate teeth** from oral environment during dental procedures. **Components**: Rubber dam sheet, punch, clamps, frame, forceps, floss. **Advantages**: **1. Moisture control**: Keeps field dry, essential for composite bonding, endodontics. **2. Infection control**: Prevents contamination from saliva, blood. **3. Soft tissue protection**: Protects cheeks, tongue, gingiva from instruments, materials. **4. Improves access and visibility**: Retracts soft tissues, better illumination. **5. Patient safety**: Prevents aspiration/ingestion of instruments, materials, irrigants. **6. Improved restoration quality**: Better bond strength for composites. **7. Patient comfort**: No constant rinsing/spitting, less gagging. **8. Increases efficiency**: Fewer interruptions. **Absolute indication**: **Endodontic treatment** (prevents aspiration of files, irrigant leakage). **Contraindications**: Severe asthma (latex allergy - use non-latex), mouth breathing difficulty.

viva.question: Differentiate between cavity liners and bases. When are they indicated?
viva.model_answer: **Cavity liner**: **Thin coating** (0.5mm or less) applied to **dentinal walls** to provide **pulpal protection**. **Materials**: **Calcium hydroxide**, **Glass ionomer**, **Resin-modified glass ionomer**, **Varnish** (copal varnish). **Functions**: (1) **Therapeutic** - stimulates secondary dentin formation (Ca(OH)₂), (2) **Protective** - bacterial seal, (3) **Palliative** - reduces sensitivity. **Cavity base**: **Thicker layer** (>0.5mm, up to 1-2mm) providing **thermal insulation** and **mechanical support**. **Materials**: **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)**, **Zinc phosphate cement**, **Glass ionomer cement**, **Resin-modified GI**. **Functions**: (1) **Thermal insulation** - protects pulp from metallic restorations, (2) **Mechanical protection** - distributes stress, (3) **Thickness replacement** - in deep cavities. **Indications**: **Deep cavities** (within 0.5mm of pulp), **Sensitive dentin**, **Under metallic restorations**. **Modern approach**: With **adhesive dentistry** (composites with bonding), extensive bases/liners often not needed. **Glass ionomer liner** releases fluoride, bonds to dentin. **Direct composite** over dentin with bonding agent eliminates need for base in many cases.

viva.question: Write a short note on POLYMERIZATION OF COMPOSITE RESIN.
viva.model_answer:
Various types of curing lights 1) High intensity quartz - tungsten-Haloges
2) Plasma arc curing (PAC) light
3) light emitting diode [IED] light
4) Argon laser curing lights
(a) HIGH INTENSITY QUARTZ - TUNGSTEN - HALOGEN.
These have an Tsed light output (>1200mW/cm2) to produce faster
curing of composite resins.
But, faster curing may result in lesser conversion of monomers in
resin matrix to polymers
Polymerization shrinkage can also be high enough to cause failure of

the bond to tooth structure
Hence high intensity QTH lights are currently available as variable

LAWS OF ACCESS CAVITY PREPARATION (3/5M) for Locating canal Orifices
Law of centrality- Floor of pulp chamber IS almost always located is the
centre of tooth at the level of CEJ
Law of CEJ- Distance from external surface of clinical crown to the wall
of pulp chamber is same throughout the tooth circumference
at the level of CEJ.
Law of concentricity - walls of pulp chamber are always concentric to
external surface reflects the anatomy of pulp chamber
Law of colour change- colour of floor of pulpchamber is darker than
the cavity walls.
Law of symmetry - Except for maxillary molars, canal the orifices are
equidistant froma line drawn in mesiodistal
direchon through the floor of pulp chamber.
Law of orifice locahon - canal orifices are located at the junchon
of floor and walls at the terminus of root
development fusion lines
